questions.question: What does LFSR stand for in the context of hardware CRC computation?
questions.explanation: LFSR stands for Linear Feedback Shift Register — a shift register circuit that computes CRC by processing one bit per clock cycle, using XOR taps at positions corresponding to the generator polynomial's non-zero coefficients.

questions.question: How many bytes does the Frame Check Sequence (FCS) occupy at the end of an IEEE 802.3 Ethernet frame?
questions.explanation: The FCS is a 32-bit (4-byte) CRC-32 value appended to every Ethernet frame. It covers from the Destination MAC address through the end of the Data/Pad field; the preamble and FCS field itself are not included in the computation.

questions.question: What is the reflected (LSB-first) form of the IEEE 802.3 CRC-32 polynomial 0x04C11DB7 used in table-driven software implementations?
questions.explanation: 0xEDB88320 is the bit-reversed (reflected) form of 0x04C11DB7. Table-driven CRC implementations use this reflected polynomial because Ethernet's serial wire protocol transmits the LSB of each byte first, so the LFSR taps must be mirrored to match that bit order.

questions.question: After the reflected IEEE 802.3 CRC-32 engine processes a complete correct frame (data bytes plus 4 FCS bytes in little-endian wire order), what fixed value does the raw accumulator hold before post-conditioning?
questions.explanation: The raw accumulator of the reflected CRC engine settles at 0xDEBB20E3 for any correctly formed frame. This is the receiver's magic constant. The often-cited textbook value 0xC704DD7B is the equivalent residue for the non-reflected (MSB-first) academic description with big-endian FCS — a different but mathematically equivalent formulation.

questions.question: Which byte range does the IEEE 802.3 CRC-32 FCS cover?
options: From the Preamble through the end of the Data/Pad field
From the Destination MAC address through the end of the Data/Pad field
From the Destination MAC address through the FCS field itself
From the EtherType/Length field through the end of the Data/Pad field
questions.explanation: The FCS covers from the Destination MAC address (the first byte after the 8-byte preamble) through the last byte of Data/Pad. The preamble and the FCS field itself are excluded. An appliance that starts CRC coverage at the wrong byte — for example including the preamble or skipping the Dst MAC — produces a valid-looking but incorrect FCS.

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'pre-conditioning' in the IEEE 802.3 CRC-32 computation?
options: It reverses the bit order of each byte before processing
It appends 32 zero bits to the message before division
It initializes the LFSR register to 0xFFFFFFFF (all ones)
It inverts the final 32-bit CRC result before appending it to the frame
questions.explanation: Pre-conditioning initializes the shift register to all-ones (0xFFFFFFFF) instead of all-zeros. This ensures that frames with extra leading zero bits prepended are detectable — without pre-conditioning, any number of leading zero bytes would leave the CRC unchanged, creating a security gap.
